# Oprah Winfrey

American television personality, actress, and entrepreneur

Top Questions

### Who is Oprah Winfrey?

Oprah Winfrey is an American [television](https://www.britannica.com/technology/television-technology) personality, actress, and entrepreneur whose syndicated daily [talk show](https://www.britannica.com/topic/talk-show) , _The Oprah Winfrey Show_ , was among the most popular of the genre. She became one of the richest and most influential women in the [United States](https://www.britannica.com/place/United-States) .

### What awards did Oprah Winfrey win?

In 2010 Oprah Winfrey was named a [Kennedy Center](https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Kennedy-Center) honoree. In 2013 she was awarded the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Presidential-Medal-of-Freedom) . She won the Cecil B. DeMille Award (a [Golden Globe](https://www.britannica.com/art/Golden-Globe-Award) for lifetime achievement) in 2018. In addition, she won numerous [Emmy Awards](https://www.britannica.com/art/Emmy-Award) for _The Oprah Winfrey Show_ and other [television](https://www.britannica.com/technology/television-technology) work.

### How did Oprah Winfrey help the world?

Oprah Winfrey engaged in numerous [philanthropic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philanthropy) activities, including the creation of Oprah’s Angel Network, which sponsors charitable initiatives worldwide. In 2007 she opened a $40 million school for disadvantaged girls in [South Africa](https://www.britannica.com/place/South-Africa) . She also became an outspoken crusader against [child abuse](https://www.britannica.com/topic/child-abuse) .

## News •

Oprah Winfrey names Maria Semple’s 'Go Gentle' as her latest book club pick • Apr. 14, 2026, 8:15 AM ET (AP)

Show less

**Oprah Winfrey** (born January 29, 1954, Kosciusko, [Mississippi](https://www.britannica.com/place/Mississippi-state) , U.S.) is an American [television](https://www.britannica.com/technology/television-technology) personality, [actress](https://www.britannica.com/art/acting) , and [entrepreneur](https://www.britannica.com/money/entrepreneurship) whose [syndicated](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/syndicated) daily [talk show](https://www.britannica.com/topic/talk-show) , _The Oprah Winfrey Show_ , revolutionized the [genre](https://www.britannica.com/art/genre-literature) . That program—as well as her hugely popular [book club](https://www.britannica.com/money/book-club) , various magazines, OWN channel, and movies—earned Winfrey the title “Queen of All Media.” These ventures also made her one of the richest and most influential women in the [United States](https://www.britannica.com/place/United-States) .

## Early life and education

Winfrey moved to [Milwaukee](https://www.britannica.com/place/Milwaukee) , [Wisconsin](https://www.britannica.com/place/Wisconsin) , at age six to live with her mother. In her early teens she was sent to [Nashville](https://www.britannica.com/place/Nashville-Tennessee) to live with her father, who proved to be a positive influence in her life. Winfrey received a full scholarship to [Tennessee State University](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Tennessee-State-University) but left to pursue a career in broadcasting; she eventually earned her degree in 1986. At age 19 she became a news anchor for the local [CBS](https://www.britannica.com/money/CBS-Broadcasting-Inc) [television](https://www.britannica.com/technology/television-technology) station, and in 1976 she was made a reporter and coanchor for the [ABC](https://www.britannica.com/money/American-Broadcasting-Company) news [affiliate](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/affiliate) in [Baltimore](https://www.britannica.com/place/Baltimore) , [Maryland](https://www.britannica.com/place/Maryland-state) . She found herself constrained by the objectivity required of news reporting, and in 1977 she became cohost of the Baltimore morning show _People Are Talking_ .

## _The Oprah Winfrey Show_ and films

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/93/5193-004-63205589/Oprah-Winfrey-1989.jpg)

[Oprah Winfrey](https://cdn.britannica.com/93/5193-004-63205589/Oprah-Winfrey-1989.jpg) Oprah Winfrey, 1989. (more)

Winfrey excelled in the casual and personal talk-show format, and in 1984 she moved to [Chicago](https://www.britannica.com/place/Chicago) to host the faltering talk show _AM Chicago_ . Winfrey’s honest and engaging personality quickly turned the program into a success, and in 1985 it was renamed _[The Oprah Winfrey Show](https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Oprah-Winfrey-Show)_ . Syndicated nationally in 1986, the program became the highest-rated television talk show in the United States and earned several [Emmy Awards](https://www.britannica.com/art/Emmy-Award) .

Britannica Quiz Who Said It? Famous Quotes Quiz

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/54/90554-050-7E28D1ED/Oprah-Winfrey-The-Color-Purple.jpg)

[_The Color Purple_](https://cdn.britannica.com/54/90554-050-7E28D1ED/Oprah-Winfrey-The-Color-Purple.jpg) Oprah Winfrey in _The Color Purple_ (1985). (more)

In 1985 Winfrey appeared in [Steven Spielberg](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Steven-Spielberg) ’s [adaptation](https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Color-Purple-film-by-Spielberg) of [Alice Walker](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Alice-Walker) ’s 1982 novel [_The Color Purple_](https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Color-Purple) . Her critically acclaimed performance led to other roles, including a performance in the television miniseries _The Women of Brewster Place_ (1989). Winfrey formed her own television production company, Harpo Productions, Inc., in 1986, and a film production company, Harpo Films, in 1990. The companies began buying film rights to literary works, including Connie May Fowler’s _Before Women Had Wings_ , which appeared in 1997 with Winfrey as both star and producer, and [Toni Morrison](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Toni-Morrison) ’s [_Beloved_](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Beloved-novel-by-Morrison) , which appeared in 1998, also with Winfrey in a starring role.

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/33/102433-050-D3BE3CBC/Oprah-Winfrey-2007.jpg)

[Oprah Winfrey, 2007](https://cdn.britannica.com/33/102433-050-D3BE3CBC/Oprah-Winfrey-2007.jpg) (more)

Winfrey later lent her voice to several animated films, including _Charlotte’s Web_ (2006) and _[The Princess and the Frog](https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Princess-and-the-Frog)_ (2009), and appeared in _[Lee Daniels’ The Butler](https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Butler-film-2013)_ (2013). _[Selma](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Selma-film)_ (2014), a film about [Martin Luther King, Jr.,](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Martin-Luther-King-Jr) that Winfrey produced and also appeared in, was nominated for an [Academy Award for best picture](https://www.britannica.com/art/Academy-Award-for-best-picture) . She subsequently starred in the [HBO](https://www.britannica.com/money/HBO) TV movie _The Immortal Life of Henrietta Lacks_ (2017), portraying the daughter of a woman whose [cancerous cells](https://www.britannica.com/science/HeLa-cell) were, [unbeknownst](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/unbeknownst) to her and her family, used in research that led to numerous scientific advances. Winfrey then appeared as Mrs. Which in the 2018 [film adaptation](https://www.britannica.com/topic/A-Wrinkle-in-Time-film) of [Madeleine L’Engle](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Madeleine-LEngle) ’s acclaimed 1962 sci-fi novel, [_A Wrinkle in Time_](https://www.britannica.com/topic/A-Wrinkle-in-Time) .

## Other activities: book club and OWN

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/38/120738-050-2DD96C55/Book-cover-Advice-Interviews-Live-Your-Best.jpg)

[book cover of _Live Your Best Life: A Treasury of Wisdom, Wit, Advice, Interviews, and Inspiration from O, the Oprah Magazine_](https://cdn.britannica.com/38/120738-050-2DD96C55/Book-cover-Advice-Interviews-Live-Your-Best.jpg) Book cover of _Live Your Best Life: A Treasury of Wisdom, Wit, Advice, Interviews, and Inspiration from O, the Oprah Magazine_ (2005). (more)

Winfrey broke new ground in 1996 by starting an on-air book club. She announced selections two to four weeks in advance and then discussed the book on her show with a select group of people. Each book chosen quickly rose to the top of the best-seller charts, and Winfrey’s effect on the publishing industry was significant. Winfrey further expanded her presence in the publishing industry with the highly successful launch of _[O, the Oprah Magazine](https://www.britannica.com/topic/O-the-Oprah-Magazine)_ in 2000 and _O at Home_ in 2004. The latter folded in 2008, and _O, the Oprah Magazine_ [ceased](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/ceased) its print publication in 2020 and was rebranded the following year. Oprah Daily became the digital product, and _O Quarterly_ , a print edition published four times a year, was launched.

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/37/120737-050-905C987B/David-Zaslav-Oprah-Winfrey-CEO-creation-Discovery.jpg)

[David Zaslav and Oprah Winfrey](https://cdn.britannica.com/37/120737-050-905C987B/David-Zaslav-Oprah-Winfrey-CEO-creation-Discovery.jpg) Oprah Winfrey and David Zaslav, president and CEO of Discovery Communications, announcing the creation of the Oprah Winfrey Network (OWN). (more)

In 1998 Winfrey expanded her media entertainment empire when she cofounded Oxygen Media, which launched a [cable television](https://www.britannica.com/technology/cable-television) network for women. In 2006 the Oprah & Friends channel debuted on [satellite radio](https://www.britannica.com/technology/satellite-radio) . She brokered a partnership with Discovery Communications in 2008, through which the [Oprah Winfrey Network](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Oprah-Winfrey-Network) (OWN) replaced the Discovery Health Channel in January 2011. In 2009 Winfrey announced that her television talk show would end in 2011; it was speculated that she would focus on OWN. The last episode of _The Oprah Winfrey Show_ aired in May 2011, and _Oprah’s Next Chapter_ , a weekly prime-time interview program on OWN, [debuted](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/debuted) in January 2012. In 2017 it was announced that Discovery was acquiring a majority share in OWN, though Winfrey would remain involved in the channel. That year she also became a special correspondent for the newsmagazine [_60 Minutes_](https://www.britannica.com/topic/60-Minutes) , which aired on [CBS](https://www.britannica.com/money/CBS-Broadcasting-Inc) .

[SUBSCRIBE](https://premium.britannica.com/premium-membership/?utm_source=premium&utm_medium=inline-cta&utm_campaign=shorter-2026)

In 2018 Winfrey announced an agreement to produce content for the Apple TV+ streaming service, and in 2020 she began producing and hosting the streaming talk show _Oprah Talks COVID-19_ . In addition, she hosted a two-part panel discussion, _Where Do We Go From Here?_ , on OWN to address the widespread anti-racism movement that arose after the on-video killing of [George Floyd](https://www.britannica.com/biography/George-Floyd) , an unarmed African American man, by police in [Minneapolis](https://www.britannica.com/place/Minneapolis) , Minnesota. In 2021 Oprah made headlines with her [candid](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/candid) interview of [Prince Harry](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Prince-Harry-duke-of-Sussex) and his wife, [Meghan Markle](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Meghan-duchess-of-Sussex) , the couple’s first since they stopped being working members of the British royal family. The much-hyped TV special aired on CBS.

## Philanthropy and books

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/64/188764-050-83575820/Oprah-Winfrey-2013.jpg)

[Oprah Winfrey](https://cdn.britannica.com/64/188764-050-83575820/Oprah-Winfrey-2013.jpg) Oprah Winfrey, 2013. (more)

Winfrey engaged in numerous philanthropic activities, including the creation of Oprah’s Angel Network, which sponsors charitable [initiatives](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/initiatives) worldwide. In 2007 she opened a $40 million school for disadvantaged girls in [South Africa](https://www.britannica.com/place/South-Africa) . She became an outspoken crusader against [child abuse](https://www.britannica.com/topic/child-abuse) and received many honors and awards from civic, philanthropic, and entertainment organizations. In 2010 she was named a [Kennedy Center](https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Kennedy-Center) honoree, and the following year she received the Jean Hersholt Humanitarian Award from the [Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Academy-of-Motion-Picture-Arts-and-Sciences) . In 2013 Winfrey was awarded the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Presidential-Medal-of-Freedom) . She won the [Cecil B. DeMille](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Cecil-B-DeMille) Award (a [Golden Globe](https://www.britannica.com/art/Golden-Globe-Award) for lifetime achievement) in 2018, and her impassioned speech—in which she called for racial and gender equality—was widely seen as one of the ceremony’s most memorable moments.

Winfrey published several self-help books, including _The Path Made Clear: Discovering Your Life’s Direction and Purpose_ (2019) and _What Happened to You?: Conversations on Trauma, Resilience, and Healing_ (2021; written with Bruce D. Perry).

**talk show** , radio or [television](https://www.britannica.com/technology/television-technology) program in which a well-known personality interviews celebrities and other guests. The late-night television programs hosted by [Johnny Carson](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Johnny-Carson) , [Jay Leno](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Jay-Leno) , [David Letterman](https://www.britannica.com/biography/David-Letterman) , and [Conan O’Brien](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Conan-OBrien) , for example, [emphasized](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/emphasized) entertainment, incorporating interludes of music or comedy. Other talk shows focused on politics ( _see_ [David Susskind](https://www.britannica.com/biography/David-Susskind) ), controversial social issues or sensationalistic topics ( [Phil Donahue](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Phil-Donahue) ), and emotional therapy ( [Oprah Winfrey](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Oprah-Winfrey) ). _See also_ Merv Griffin; [Larry King](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Larry-King) ; [Jack Paar](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Jack-Paar) ; [Jimmy Kimmel](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Jimmy-Kimmel) ; [Jimmy Fallon](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Jimmy-Fallon) ; [Stephen Colbert](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Stephen-Colbert) .
